data.title: What to watch as Lisa Cook heads to Supreme Court
data.description:
Close

Thank you for signing up!

Subscribe to more newsletters here

The latest in politics and policy. Direct to your inbox. Sign up for the The Gavel newsletter

President Trump’s power over the Federal Reserve goes before the Supreme Court Wednesday in a blockbuster review of the central bank governors’ firing protections. 

As the president wages war against the Fed for not lowering interest rates quicker, Trump has looked to sack Fed governor Lisa Cook by accusing her of mortgage fraud, which she denies. 

Lower courts sided with Cook. The justices have punted a decision for months on Trump’s emergency appeal until they hold oral arguments, which are set to kick off at 10 a.m. EST. 

Here’s what to watch for as the argument gets underway: 

How the justices interpret ‘for cause’ 

The Federal Reserve Act of 1913 only permits the president to remove Fed governors “for cause.” 

The statute does not explicitly define “cause.” Wednesday’s case turns on how the justices interpret it. 

Everyone concedes that disagreeing with a governor’s votes on interest rates isn’t sufficient. Beyond that, the sides diverge. 

The Trump administration contends that “for cause” gives the president broad discretion to fire Fed governors for reasons related to their conduct, ability, fitness or competence. 

If the justices agree, the administration says it’s an easy case and that the mortgage fraud accusations against Cook easily pass the test. 

Cook’s lawyers push back. They say Congress intended “for cause” to be comparable to the standard in place at a handful of other independent agencies, which spell out inefficiency, neglect or malfeasance as the three preconditions to firing. 

Watch to see if the justices agree with the Trump administration’s proposal, or if they agree with the concerns of Cook that it would imperil the Fed’s independence. 

Does Cook’s due process argument gain steam 

Regardless of whether Trump had valid “cause,” Cook will advance several other arguments Wednesday. 

She asserts that due process protections under the Constitution and federal law guaranteed her a hearing to deny the mortgage fraud accusations.  

“She unequivocally does, and she is prepared to refute the allegations in an appropriate forum,” Cook’s lawyers wrote. 

The Trump administration says no such hearing is legally required and that Cook has not explained what it would look like or how it would make any difference. 

Additionally, the administration will try to convince the justices that even if she was wrongfully removed, the lower courts had no power to grant an injunction reinstating Cook as the litigation plays out. 

If the justices tip their hand on FTC decision 

The case comes to the court weeks after the justices heard arguments on Trump’s power to fire Rebecca Slaughter, a member of the Federal Trade Commission (FTC). 

The cases both involve the bounds of Trump’s presidential power, but they raise distinct issues. 

At the Fed, Trump purports to have valid cause in sacking Cook.  

But at the FTC, the president does not. Instead, he argues Slaughter’s firing protections infringe on the separation of powers — a broad assertion of presidential power that appears to have drawn sympathy with the Supreme Court’s conservative majority. 

The Trump administration has not made that argument for the Fed, which the justices have signaled has special status that may justify its protections. 

Under the court’s typical procedures, the justices would’ve taken an initial vote on Slaughter’s case in the days following the December argument. The opinion would’ve been assigned, and drafting would commence. 

Watch on Wednesday to see if the justices’ comments tip their hand on what they plan to do in Slaughter’s case. 

How Powell’s attendance impacts the case 

Wednesday’s argument only directly concerns Cook’s firing, but watch to see if the justices connect it to Trump’s broader threats against the central bank for not lowering interest rates quicker. 

Fed Chair Jerome Powell will attend Wednesday’s argument at the Supreme Court, a source familiar with his plans tells our colleague Sylvan Lane, The Hill’s business editor. 

For months, Trump has mused about firing Powell. But the president has yet to do so as the clock ticks closer to May, when Powell’s term as chair ends (he may remain on the board until 2028).  

In the lead-up to Wednesday’s argument, things took a turn. The Justice Department issued a subpoena to the Fed, bringing its criminal investigation into Powell to light. 

The investigation centers on congressional testimony Powell gave concerning the Fed’s multi-billion-dollar renovation of its headquarters, which has faced significant cost overruns. Powell denies wrongdoing and has cast the investigation as an attempt to influence the central bank’s monetary policy. 

As the Fed’s chair, Powell is formally named as a defendant in Cook’s lawsuit. 

His attendance makes him the elephant in the room. Government officials typically sit near the front of the public gallery, which is in the back half of the courtroom behind the bar section.  

We’ll be in the room Wednesday to watch the justices — and Powell. So stay tuned. 

Welcome to The Gavel, The Hill’s weekly courts newsletter from Ella Lee and Zach Schonfeld. Reach out to us on X (@ByEllaLee, @ZachASchonfeld) or Signal (elee.03, zachschonfeld.48). Sign up here or in the box below:   

IN FOCUS

Looking back at 1 year of Trump v. the courts 

President Trump entered the second year of his White House term this week. 

As his first year rapidly remade Washington, it flooded courts across the country. 

Democratic state attorneys general, legal groups formed to oppose Trump, civil rights organizations and more asked judges hundreds of times to to rule pieces of Trump’s agenda unlawful — and halt them in the meantime. 

Lawsuits began flying while Trump was still in the Capitol Rotunda for his inauguration ceremony. 

Within minutes of Chief Justice John Roberts administering the oath, three lawsuits sought to dismantle the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) before it even got started.  

They claimed DOGE was a federal advisory committee that wasn’t following transparency requirements. No judge has yet to endorse the argument, and some of the cases were later dropped. 

Still, it became the first of hundreds of legal battlefronts to come.  

And in the early months of the administration, challenges to DOGE dominated. 

Some contested the administration’s assertion that Elon Musk was not formally in charge of the group. Others sought to subject DOGE to the Freedom of Information Act. But by and large, the cases concerned efforts to implant DOGE personnel at agencies across the government and allegations they were violating the Privacy Act. 

Government unions led the charge in many of the cases. And as they would go on to do on issue after issue, left-leaning legal groups like Democracy Forward and Democracy Defenders Fund represented many of the plaintiffs in court. 

And within months, they secured dozens of injunctions from federal district judges, stymying Trump’s agenda. Temporarily. 

Repeatedly over the past year, the administration filed emergency appeals all the way to the Supreme Court, almost always emerging successful. 

In total, the administration brought 32 Supreme Court emergency appeals. That’s more than former President Biden’s Justice Department filed during his entire four years in office. 

Trump’s critics say the barrage reflects how he is acting lawlessly. 

California Attorney General Rob Bonta (D), speaking Tuesday at an event celebrating the 54 lawsuits his office has brought against Trump, summed up the president’s first year as a “sweeping, often illegal campaign to remake America.” 

“We said then if he breaks the law, we will take him to court. We are here today to say we have fulfilled that promise,” Bonta said. 

The president’s legal allies push back, suggesting the nonstop pace reveals how lower judges have overstepped their authority by granting one injunction after another.  

The real score is the administration’s record at the high court, those allies argue. 

The exact tally depends on how you count a few mixed rulings, but on our scorecard, the administration has lost only four cases and emerged victorious in roughly 20. 

Among others, that list includes greenlighting the administration’s efforts to fire more than 16,000 probationary employees, ban transgender troops from serving openly in the military, revoke temporary legal protections extended to more than 300,000 Venezuelans and parole for 532,000 people from Latin American countries, freeze $65 million in teacher preparation grants and lay off roughly half the Education Department 

Only in a few cases, like Trump’s bid to deploy the National Guard in the Chicago area, did the court rule against the president. 

What’s in store for his second year? Here are five issues that will define the legal landscape: 

  1. Does Trump’s string of Supreme Court victories continue as the justices begin handing down final decisions on the merits of Trump’s policies — like his sweeping tariffs and efforts to control independent agencies — rather than preliminary decisions on the emergency docket? 
  1. Will the Justice Department criminally indict more prominent Trump foes, like Powell, Sen. Adam Schiff (D-Calif.), Sen. Mark Kelly (D-Ariz.) and former Special Counsel Jack Smith? 
  1. Will the Supreme Court rein in judges who’ve granted nationwide relief to plaintiffs by signing off on class actions in the wake of the justices clawing back universal injunctions? 
  1. Will Trump’s rapid pace of executive orders, which now exceeds 200, slow down? 
  1. Will Trump invoke the Insurrection Act? 

Alien Enemies Act back in court 

One of the most prominent legal battles of Trump’s first year back in the White House returns to the courtroom later this week. 

Last March, Trump invoked the Alien Enemies Act of 1798 to swiftly deport alleged Venezuelan gang members. 

It began with sending dozens of migrants to a Salvadoran megaprison. Another wave in April was underway when the Supreme Court intervened in the middle of the night to halt the flights at the pleas of the American Civil Liberties Union. 

The deportations became a prominent sign last spring of Trump’s aggressive immigration agenda.  

In the months since, the legal battle has calmed in some respects. It’s about to return to the spotlight. 

On Thursday, all 17 active judges on the 5th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals will convene at the federal courthouse in New Orleans to hear oral arguments on whether Trump legally invoked the Alien Enemies Act. 

It’s slated to be a blockbuster session that appears likely destined for the Supreme Court. More than a half-dozen other legal challenges to Trump’s use of the law are on hold, all awaiting word from the 5th Circuit. 

The Alien Enemies Act allows the president to summarily deport migrants when a foreign nation perpetrates an “invasion” or predatory “incursion.” It has only been invoked three prior times: the War of 1812 and World Wars I and II. 

Trump claims Tren de Aragua is perpetrating an invasion against the U.S., and he has tied the transnational criminal gang to the Venezuelan government. 

In April, the Supreme Court ruled that migrants detained under the Alien Enemies Act must receive notice and an opportunity to challenge their removal before they are deported. 

That decision leaves several questions unanswered that have been simmering ever since. The 5th Circuit will get the next bite at the apple Thursday. 

American Civil Liberties Union lawyer Lee Gelernt will argue the Alien Enemies Act requires a a declaration of war or a military attack for the president to invoke the law.  

The statute requires the attack be committed by a foreign nation, and Gelernt will make the case that Trump can’t tie his concerns about Tren de Aragua to deposed President Nicolás Maduro’s regime. 

And even if the president did lawfully invoke the statute, Gelernt will argue that migrants should be afforded at least 30-days’ notice to ensure the Trump administration can’t deport them without judicial review. 

The Trump administration will be represented by Drew Ensign, who leads immigration litigation for the Justice Department. He will assert that the Alien Enemies Act compels the courts to provide the president extreme deference.  

Tren de Aragua has perpetrated an invasion “by any metric” and is sufficiently connected to Maduro’s government, administration wrote in court filings. The administration has also argued requiring 30-days’ notice is unwarranted.  

SIDEBAR

5 top docket updates    

  1. No tariffs: The Supreme Court released two more batches of opinions this past week, but still no decision in the highly anticipated battle over Trump’s tariffs. The next opinion release day remains unclear, but the justices are not currently scheduled to take the bench again until after President’s Day. 
  1. California map upheld: A divided three-judge panel upheld California’s new congressional map set to add several Democratic-leaning seats as part of the mid-decade redistricting war. The California GOP has already provided notice it is appealing directly to the Supreme Court. 
  1. Offshore wind projects resume: Separate judges issued orders allowing construction to resume on the Empire Wind and Coastal Virginia offshore wind projects 
  1. SCOTUS says candidates can challenge mail ballot deadline: The Supreme Court ruled Rep. Mike Bost (R-Ill.) has legal standing to challenge Illinois’ practice of accepting mail ballots that arrive after Election Day. The justices will review the legality of the practice later this term. 
  1. College basketball indictment: The Justice Department unsealed an indictment against 26 people over an alleged point-shaving scheme to fix college basketball games. It marks the latest federal charges against athletes over betting accusations. 

In other news    

  • Job posting — but is there an opening?: The chief judge in the Eastern District of Virginia has begun soliciting applications to become the district’s interim U.S. attorney, the post that the Trump administration claims Lindsey Halligan occupies. It’s the latest twist in the battle over Halligan’s disqualification.  judge disqualified her. 
  • Billboard top artist: Leonard Jacoby, a cofounder of the law firm that helped transform mass legal advertising and make the legal system more available to everyday Americans, such as the use of billboards, died at 83, the Los Angeles Times reported.  
  • Kiefer Sutherland arrested: “Designated Survivor” star Keifer Sutherland was arrested in Hollywood over allegations he assaulted and threatened a ride-share driver, according to NBC News. 

ORDER LIST

Cases the Supreme Court is taking up — or passing on — this term.  

IN: Roundup weed killer, geofence warrants 

The court took up four new cases at Friday’s conference, perhaps the final cases that will be argued this term. 

First, the court agreed to hear Bayer’s appeal to limit liability for its Roundup weed killer, which has come under thousands of lawsuits alleging it causes cancer and did not include a proper warning label.  

Bayer argues the state lawsuits are preempted by federal law, which does not require the warning. The case is Monsanto Company v. Durnell. 

In Chatrie v. United States, the Supreme Court took up its next major Fourth Amendment case. 

The justices will review whether geofence warrants, which allow law enforcement to seize information on cellphones online at a location during a specific time window, violate constitutional protections against unreasonable searches. 

The court will also review an employment retirement plan case interpreting the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974, (ERISA).  

In Anderson v. Intel Corporation Investment Policy Committee, the justices will examine whether plaintiffs alleging their employer’s plan underperformed must show a “meaningful benchmark,” which would make it more difficult to bring ERISA lawsuits. 

Finally, the court agreed to hear Hikma Pharmaceuticals USA v. Amarin Pharma. 

The fight between the two pharma companies poses broad implications for so-called “skinny labels,” which allow generic drugmakers to bring products to market faster by carving out a product’s brand-name, patented use on the label. 

OUT: SCOTUS fee waivers 

Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson, the court’s juniormost liberal justice, has thoughts on how the court handles filings from incarcerated defendants. 

The Supreme Court chooses what cases it hears. But anyone can try their luck, so long as they submit a $300 filing fee and pay to print 40 bound copies (yes, this is still a requirement in 2026, and Jackson noted it’s usually the much higher cost). 

The justices routinely waive the fee for poor incarcerated defendants who can’t afford it. But if the defendant then repeatedly tries to file frivolous litigation, the court cuts them off. 

The court did so Tuesday for a handful of defendants, including Danny Howell, who was sentenced to 70 years in prison after being convicted of child molestation and other charges. The clerk will no longer accept “any further petitions in noncriminal matters” from Howell unless he pays the fee.  

“Even if Howell were to identify meritorious grounds for habeas relief or wanted to bring a justifiable challenge to his conditions of confinement, he will now be prevented from doing so unless he pays the filing fee—no matter what,” Jackson wrote in dissent. 

“A categorical, forward-looking filing bar is a questionable restriction as to any litigant who cannot afford to pay a filing fee. For me, it is an intolerable one as to incarcerated individuals.” 

ON THE DOCKET

Don’t be surprised if additional hearings are scheduled throughout the week. But here’s what we’re watching for now:  

Today: 

  • The Supreme Court will hear oral arguments over Trump’s efforts to fire Federal Reserve board of governors member Lisa Cook. The case is Trump v. Cook.  

Thursday: 

  • Former special counsel Jack Smith will testify publicly before the House Judiciary Committee. 
  • The entire U.S. Court of Appeals for the 5th Circuit is set to hear oral arguments in a challenge two Venezuelan nationals, represented by the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU), brought to Trump’s invocation of the Alien Enemies Act. 
  • The U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit is set to hear oral arguments in transgender service members’ and prospective service members’ challenge to Trump’s order banning transgender troops from serving in the military. 
  • A federal judge in Washington state is set to hold a summary judgment hearing in 16 Democratic state attorneys general’s challenge to the Education Department’s discontinuation of mental health funding under the Mental Health Service Professional Demonstration Grant Program (MHSP) for low-income/rural schools and Mental Health Services Grant Program (SBMH). 
  • A federal judge in Washington, D.C., is set to hold a preliminary injunction hearing over Trump’s bid to renovate the White House ballroom.  

Friday: 

  • A federal judge in New York is set to hold a preliminary injunction hearing in five states’ challenge to the freezing of $10 billion in federal funds by the Administration for Children and Families (ACF). 
  • A federal judge in New York has ordered an Altoona Police Department officer to testify about the department’s arrest procedures as the judge mulls Luigi Mangione’s request to suppress key evidence at his criminal trial. 

Monday: 

  • The Supreme Court will announce orders.  

Tuesday: 

  • The Senate Judiciary Subcommittee on the Constitution will hold a hearing on Trump’s efforts to restrict birthright citizenship for children born to parents in the country unlawfully. 

WHAT WE’RE READING

  1. The Wall Street Journal’s Michelle Hackman, Kris Maher and Brenna T. Smith: The Standoff That Has Turned Minnesota Into a Tinderbox 
  1. The National Law Journal’s Jimmy Hoover: “’The Technology Is There’: Supreme Court Practitioners Quietly Embracing AI” 
  1. CourtWatch: The Rabbit Hole: ‘Metastasizing Evil’ 
  1. Politico E&E News’s Niina H. Farah: Legal tests await Trump’s offshore energy agenda in 2026 
  1. ABC News’s Matt Rivers, Janice McDonald, and Armando Garcia: Lawyers allege Dept. of Homeland Security is denying legal counsel to Minnesota detainees

data.title: Trump must mend, not widen, the rift between MAGA and pro-lifers
data.description:
For most of my adult life, I wasn’t very pro-life. I never thought abortion was a great thing, especially when used as birth control for irresponsible people, but I was always more interested in other issues.

And, if I’m being brutally honest — though it was always an abstract fear — there was that dread in the back of every young man’s head that someone they’re dating would become pregnant and, well, you wouldn’t necessarily want to deny that woman her “rights,” would you?

I never wanted kids when I was younger. I can’t imagine any woman wanting to have a child with my younger self, either. But eventually, everyone grows up and their opinions change. Now, I’m married, and I have two great kids. I went from living in fear of children as a concept and never wanting any to wishing I could have a dozen.

That brings me to the annual March for Life. What used to be a commemoration of the Roe v Wade decision is now a celebration of its demise and the continued fight to protect life across the country. It’s coming up on January 23.

As that date approaches, it is important to note that there is a growing divide between the pro-life movement and President Trump’s MAGA movement. Whether that gap will continue to grow or whether Trump will close it remains to be seen. But with the midterms looming, this problem cannot be ignored.

Wherever you fall on the issue of protecting unborn human life, the pro-life coalition will be a crucial part of any coalition making it possible for Republicans to maintain control of Congress after this year’s election. 

The Daily Wire recently reported that a “coalition of pro-life leaders urged the Trump administration on Tuesday to take action against ‘attacks on the unborn and most vulnerable’ they said were coming from inside the White House.” Their issue was that the Food and Drug Administration had “approved a generic form of the abortion pill in October and maintained the mail-order abortion regulations.”

This move caused Sen. Josh Hawley (R-Mo.) to say he had “lost confidence in the leadership at the FDA.”

FDA Commissioner Marty Makary has even pushed off past the midterms publishing any safety reviews of the abortion pill. If you wanted to actively antagonize pro-life voters, this is what you would do.

Add to this the fact that one of Makary’s most-trusted deputies, Vinay Prasad, has publicly declared himself “a Bernie Sanders liberal” and “pro universal health care. Pro wealth tax. Pro choice. Etc.” It only adds to the rift.

Then there’s the fact that Trump recently called for Republicans to end Obamacare subsidies by giving the money directly to the American people — but “you have to be a little flexible on Hyde, you know that. You gotta be a little flexible, you gotta work something.” The Hyde Amendment is the appropriations provision that prevents federal money from being used to fund abortion. Trump’s call for “flexibility” did not and does not sit well with pro-life groups.

Activist Lila Rose has posted to her X account, “Trump calls to make the Hyde Amendment ‘flexible,’ potentially opening the door to taxpayer-funded abortion. The GOP must stand firm for human life. No exceptions. No inch given. If you sacrifice Hyde, you sacrifice innocent human children.”

For the pro-life coalition, overturning Roe was not a finish line — it was a checkpoint. It is a victory to be celebrated, but the fight continues. It is doubtful that the movement’s activists thought that fight would come at them from leadership at the FDA or the White House.

For Republicans to win in November, the entire coalition will need to pull together to overcome Democrats. To alienate one of the most motivated and grateful wings of that coalition is not smart politics. It will make the fight unnecessarily more difficult.

The people for whom abortion is a priority that moves their vote will always vote against Republicans, as they are pot-committed to the concept and will not waver. Voters for whom life is the issue that moves their vote, on the other hand, will not vote for someone wavering on abortion — either in a clinic or by pill. The White House needs to be unambiguous on the issue, lest it lose a crucial voting bloc in a year where every vote will matter.

Derek Hunter is host of The Derek Hunter Show, weekdays from 3 p.m. to 6 p.m. on WMAL in Washington. He is a former staffer for the late Sen. Conrad Burns (R-Mont.).

data.title: Trump arrives in Switzerland after Air Force One electrical issue
data.description:
President Trump touched down in Switzerland on Wednesday nearly two hours behind schedule, after a “minor electrical issue” forced Air Force One to turn back to Washington around 30 minutes into the flight to switch planes.

Trump, flying on a smaller plane typically reserved for domestic travel, landed in Zurich shortly after 12:30 p.m. local time, or 6:30 a.m. EST. He is heading to the World Economic Forum in Davos, where he is scheduled to deliver remarks Wednesday morning.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt dismissed the issue, joking to reporters on board that the controversial Qatari plane gifted to the U.S. was now sounding “much better.”

The president’s highly anticipated arrival overseas comes as tensions with European allies have escalated over Trump’s insistence on taking control of Greenland, the semiautonomous Danish territory in the Arctic. The president over the weekend announced new tariffs on Denmark and several other European allies over their resistance to his plans to acquire the island.

His Wednesday morning address is expected to focus on his “America First” and economic agenda, as well as his plans for the U.S. to dominate the Western Hemisphere, a White House official told reporters on the plane to Davos. Trump may also talk about his effort to acquire Greenland and his future plans for Venezuela after the U.S. captured its leader Nicolás Maduro.

The president, after his morning remarks, will meet with foreign leaders, before participating in a reception with business leaders.

The White House official said he is expected to have about five separate meetings with individual leaders during the trip. The official would not disclose the leaders.

U.S. and European Union trade representatives met on the sidelines of the international forum on Wednesday, according to Maroš Šefčovič, the EU trade representative.

Trump will also address his economic agenda next week in Iowa and is expected to travel more domestically ahead of the 2026 midterms, White House chief of staff Susie Wiles told reporters before landing in Switzerland.

data.title: Leavitt jokes Qatari plane sounding 'much better' after Air Force One tech issue
data.description:
White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt joked to reporters that the Qatari plane gifted to the U.S. was sounding “much better” following a tech issue that forced Air Force One to return to Joint Base Andrews on Tuesday. 

The incident took place as President Trump was en route to Davos, Switzerland for the World Economic Forum. 

Leavitt confirmed that a “minor electrical issue” was found after takeoff and, out of an abundance of caution, the plane returned to JBA.

Trump, his staff and the press pool then got on a smaller plane, later landing in Zurich early Wednesday afternoon, local time

Trump is expected to give a speech at Davos and meet with a host of foreign leaders on matters concerning Greenland and the creation of a Board of Peace intended to oversee Gaza.

Trump accepted the gift jet, a $400 million luxury Boeing 747-8 that the Qatari royal family had previously used. Dubbed a “flying palace” the gift caused controversy at the time with critics saying it violated the emoluments clause and presents conflicts of interest.

The plane does not appear to currently be in use. Chief Pentagon spokesperson Sean Parnell previously said the Department of Defense will “work to ensure proper security measures and functional-mission requirements are considered for an aircraft used to transport the President of the United States.”

data.title: Live updates: Trump to address World Economic Forum as he digs in on Greenland
data.description:
President Trump on Wednesday morning will speak at the World Economic Forum, where he’ll face off with European leaders amid his push to seize Greenland.

Here’s what to expect from the president’s trip to Davos, Switzerland, as some Trump allies on Capitol Hill suggest the president should back off his efforts to acquire the island nation.

Trump arrived in Switzerland earlier this morning after an electrical issue forced Air Force One to turn back to Joint Base Andrews to board a new aircraft.

In Washington, the Supreme Court will hear oral arguments in the case regarding Trump’s firing of Federal Reserve board member Lisa Cook. Here’s what you need to know.

The House on Wednesday, meanwhile, will have votes to hold former President Clinton and Secretary of State Hillary Clinton in contempt, as Republicans seek to shift the heat over the Jeffrey Epstein files away from the conference.

Congress is also staring down a Jan. 31 government shutdown deadline as Democrats threaten to derail Department of Homeland Security funding negotiations over recent federal immigration enforcement action in Minnesota.

Follow live below.

data.title: Watch live: Trump gives speech in Davos amid Greenland push
data.description:
President Trump will give a speech Wednesday morning at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland — bringing him face-to-face with European allies as tensions build over his push to acquire Greenland.

While Trump is likely to tout how his policies have made the U.S. economy stronger, the high-stakes meetings could play a significant role in determining the future of Washington’s involvement in the Danish territory. He is expected to talk with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte and other allies during his trip, after threatening over the weekend to slap tariffs on nations that do not support his administration’s takeover of the Arctic island.

The president is also waiting for answers from many world leaders on who will join his Board of Peace in Gaza.

The address is scheduled to begin at 8:30 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Trump's unhinged Norway letter is a danger to national security
data.description:
President Trump’s letter this week to Norway’s prime minister — essentially demanding the handover of Greenland by a third NATO country, Denmark — is not merely inappropriate and undignified. It is incoherent, legally reckless, and hugely destabilizing to an alliance critical to our security.

No previous president of either party could have written such a document. The letter reads like a stream of grievances, misconceptions, and threats. It seriously calls into question whether Trump understands the powers and responsibilities of his office.

It begins with the extraordinary claim that because Norway “decided not to give me the Nobel Peace Prize for having stopped 8 Wars PLUS,” Trump longer feels “an obligation to think purely of Peace.” He may now focus instead on “what is good and proper” for the U.S.

Every part of this sentence is troubling. Norway does not even decide who receives the Nobel Peace Prize; an independent committee does. The prize is not a transactional reward owed personally to Trump as a bargaining chip. The claim of having stopped eight wars or more is also unsubstantiated and unrecognized by any serious international body.

More disturbing still is the implication that American restraint and responsibility are contingent upon other diplomats’ aggrandizement of Trump.

The suggestion that he no longer feels obligated to “think purely of Peace” reads like a threat. The implication that, until now, he had not pursued what is good and proper for America is true, but presumably not intended — it occupies the dark space between admission and absurdity.

Turning to Greenland, Trump then writes that Denmark “cannot protect that land from Russia or China,” and questions why Denmark has a “right of ownership” just because “a boat landed there hundreds of years ago.” He adds that “we had boats landing there, also.”

This passage collapses under even minimal scrutiny. It falsely implies that Denmark stands alone in defending Greenland. In fact Greenland is part of the NATO security umbrella, and any attack on it would engage the collective defense obligations of the alliance, including the U.S. It reduces sovereignty to military capability, suggesting that the right to territory flows from who can “protect” it, rather than from law, governance, international recognition, and the will of its people.

That logic mirrors the very arguments the U.S. rejects when advanced by Russia in Ukraine or by China in the South China Sea. If sovereignty is determined by power rather than law, then America forfeits its moral and legal basis for opposing territorial aggression elsewhere. The damage invites precisely the behavior Washington claims to resist.

The letter also ignores Greenland’s actual status. It is not a colonial outpost but an autonomous territory, with its own elected government and explicit rights of self-determination.

Trump then declares that he has “done more for NATO than any person since its founding” and that now “NATO should do something for the United States.” This is historically indefensible. NATO is the product of decades of collective effort by American and European leaders alike. Trump himself has repeatedly weakened the alliance, calling it “obsolete,” questioning its mutual defense obligations, and now, astoundingly, refusing to rule out using force against a member state. The suggestion that NATO owes him personally is just that much more absurd.

Besides, NATO already “does something” for the U.S., which currently operates or has access to 27 military bases in NATO countries — bases that former Supreme Allied Commander Chris Cavoli, an American general, once called “the first line of defense of our homeland.”

There has also been a huge rise in real annual defense spending by non-U.S. alliance members, from $289 billion in 2014 to $609 billion in 2025. Data from the 2020 to 2024 period also indicates that 64 percent of arms purchases by European NATO members came from U.S. firms.

NATO’s mutual defense clause, Article Five, has been invoked only once in history — for the U.S. after 9/11, freeing up NATO assets for the theaters in Iraq and Afghanistan.

Despite Trump’s rhetoric, Americans seem to grasp all of this. In recent polling, 68 percent said they supported the alliance.

Trump’s letter culminates in perhaps its most extraordinary claim: “The World is not secure unless we have Complete and Total Control of Greenland.” No serious strategic doctrine supports this idea. The U.S. does not control every region vital to global stability, nor could it.

Security comes from alliances, law, deterrence, and cooperation, not possession. Greenland is already inside a collective defense system that includes the U.S. America already has extensive military access there through agreements with Denmark.

The letter’s placement of personal grievance above national interest, its erratic capitalization, breathless tone, self-referential language, and its performative signoff (“Thank you! President DJT”) all project not authority but instability. As for its logic, such as it is, it may even embolden China and Russia and legitimize their future aggressions.

It is for moments like this that the Twenty-Fifth Amendment exists. We’re probably not there yet, but soon even Republicans in the Cabinet and Congress may have to start thinking about putting national interest first.

Colonel (Ret.) Robert Hamilton is president of the Delphi Global Research Center and former head of Eurasia Research at the Foreign Policy Research Institute. Dan Perry is the former chief editor of the Associated Press in Europe, Africa and the Middle East, former chairman of the Foreign Press Association in Jerusalem, and the author of two books.

data.title: Don't just increase proteins in the Food Pyramid — help make them healthier
data.description:
On Jan. 7, the U.S. Department of Agriculture and the Department of Health and Human Services released its Dietary Guidelines for Americans for 2025 through 2030. Secretaries Brooke Rollins and Robert F. Kennedy Jr. billed the rollout as “the most significant reset of federal nutrition in our nation’s history.”

In some respects, they’re right. The new guidance is clear: Eat real food. Cut added sugar. Avoid ultra-processed products loaded with additives.

But the new food pyramid makes one high stakes change that could undercut its own promise. It urges Americans to eat substantially more meat and dairy, while declining to distinguish between animal products from healthy sources and those produced in industrial systems.

Putting steak and cheese above refined carbohydrates, generally speaking, sounds sensible. But without a shift in how animal agriculture works, increasing U.S. consumption of meat and dairy also means ramping up dangerous antibiotics, pesticide-saturated feed, and pollution-heavy waste systems.

The problem isn’t the focus on meat or dairy per se. The problem is where most of it comes from. In the U.S., much of our animal food supply depends on large confinement facilities — concentrated animal feeding operations built for maximum output at minimum cost. Producers rely on routine antibiotics and other drugs to keep disease in check, creating further risks of antibiotic resistance that threaten human health.

Feed often comes from corn and soy grown in pesticide-heavy monocultures and shipped in at scale. And the waste doesn’t disappear: manure from these concentrated animal feeding operations comes in volumes that can pollute local air, contaminate streams and groundwater, and degrade surrounding land. In Ohio, we saw huge algal blooms choke off aquatic life in Lake Erie that were directly tied to a major animal feeding operations in the northwest part of the state.

The unhealthy side of that production model shows up in the food, too. Animal products can carry what the system uses — chemical residues from feed and persistent pollutants that move through soil and water. Some contaminants concentrate in animal tissue, especially fat. When you eat higher on the food chain, you don’t just get more protein — you also absorb more of the system’s chemical footprint.

All this might seem like a niche concern for Whole Foods moms, but it isn’t. It is about what ends up in kids’ school meals. It’s about what farmworkers breathe and touch, and what runs off fields into streams and wells that provide our water. Farm states like Iowa have faced rapid rises in cancer rates in recent years, underscoring the risks of the chemical-intensive industrial system to the people who grow our food.  

If the federal government wants Americans to eat more animal-based foods, it should clarify the importance of decent standards. In some cases, that means organic or pasture-raised. But it can also mean a broad range of regenerative practices like cover-crops, diversified rotations, or managed grazing.

Of course, the problem is tat all of this is expensive, especially amid rising food prices that are squeezing Americans’ budgets.

That’s why a serious strategy to “make America healthy again” should focus on helping farmers reduce dependence on chemical inputs and increase affordable access to healthy, minimally-processed foods. It should reimagine the system so that any agricultural subsidy steers us toward wellness and ultimately reduces healthcare costs.

Rollins and Kennedy are touting their new initiatives around “regenerative agriculture,” but the Trump administration has also cut the capacity that helps farmers transition away from destructive industrial practices. There are fewer experts available now to help farmers plant cover crops, improve grazing, reduce chemical inputs, and rebuild soil health.

At the same time, Republicans in Congress have been seeking to shield pesticide companies from lawsuits and to weaken oversight of “forever chemicals” harmful to human health. And the Trump administration is staffing agencies, including the EPA, with lobbyists from the pesticide industry. 

In essence, the Trump administration is recommending that people eat healthy food while eliminating the rules and tools that make that possible. Policymakers need a better strategy. 

First, if guidelines recommend more meat and dairy, they should also say what kind: products raised with fewer chemicals, less routine drug use, and better land and water practices. Keep it simple: If you eat more animal foods, do your best to choose sources that reduce chemical exposure and pollution.

Second, make those choices cheaper and easier. The government already shapes markets through what it buys and funds. But the Agriculture Department’s purchases still overwhelmingly favor big industrial agriculture and help concentrate power among megaproducers. It should strengthen procurement standards, including for school meals and other public programs.

Third, align farm policy with nutrition goals. Shift subsidies and incentives away from chemical-dependent commodity production and toward diversified farms, regional processing, and supply chains that let small and mid-sized producers compete. 

American nutrition needed a reset. Inverting the food pyramid can be a positive development. But if we want a “real food” system, we need to invest in building one.

Tim Ryan is a former 10-term Democratic member of the U.S. House from Ohio. Justin Talbot Zorn served as legislative director for three members of Congress and is a senior adviser at the Center for Economic and Policy Research in Washington.

data.title: Trump threatens to upend GOP agenda, Senate race with Louisiana endorsement
data.description:
President Trump is giving Senate GOP leaders a major headache with his decision to endorse Rep. Julia Letlow’s (R-La.) Senate campaign over Sen. Bill Cassidy’s (R-La.), tossing fresh uncertainty over key parts of their agenda and upending the race in a red state.  

Trump preemptively backed Letlow’s Senate campaign over the weekend. The move roiled the contest for a safe GOP Senate seat, put him at odds with Senate GOP leaders and created fresh tumult for the party’s forthcoming legislative push. 

Headlining that effort would be a second partisan package passed with only GOP votes and a potential health care bill, both of which would likely run through the Health, Education, Labor and Pensions (HELP) Committee — which Cassidy chairs. This year could offer Republicans’ last chance to usher through a bill with only GOP support if the party loses the House in November. 

“Did anyone explain that to the president?” one GOP operative with knowledge of the Trump-Cassidy relationship asked. “Cassidy as a free agent is one of the scarier ones in the Senate Republican Conference.” 

The operative noted that the HELP panel not only features the two-term lawmaker, but also Sens. Susan Collins (R-Maine) and Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska) — two members who are notoriously cool to the president’s wishes. 

“To get a majority with Dems on things to f— the president [on the committee], it’s not complicated,” they added. 

Trump has zeroed in on Cassidy for years, especially after the Louisiana Republican voted to convict him in his impeachment trial over his actions on Jan. 6, 2021. 

Cassidy, a physician, has worked to make amends and stay in Trump’s good graces after the president was reelected. He notably voted to confirm Robert F. Kennedy Jr. to lead the Department of Health and Human Services despite his reservations.

Cassidy was already facing several primary challenges from his right flank prior to Letlow’s entrance

GOP leaders are continuing to back the incumbent Republican. Senate Majority Whip John Barrasso (R-Wyo.) told reporters on Tuesday that he’s backed Cassidy “from the beginning” and that plan isn’t changing

“We’re going to do everything we can to build the majority,” Barrasso said when asked how much of a “headache” the president’s endorsement is for the party. 

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and the National Republican Senatorial Committee are also fully behind Cassidy, with Thune appearing in Louisiana alongside the senator for an event to promote the GOP’s tax cut bill and a fundraiser last Thursday. 

The appearance came a day before Trump phoned Thune to inform him of his plan to support Letlow, who won her seat in 2021 after her late husband passed away. Chatter had been percolating about her potential entrance dating back to the fall. 

According to a source familiar with the leader, Thune “did what he could do” to try to sway Trump to Cassidy’s side, including laying out the Louisiana senator’s accomplishments. He also pointed out the narrow margins Republicans have to work with on various bills and nominations and that “having someone like Cassidy on our side is important” to secure many of Trump’s priorities. 

A bipartisan group of lawmakers have been working toward a compromise bill to extend the enhanced ObamaCare tax credits, but they have run into a brick wall in recent weeks that is making members pessimistic that a deal can come together. 

GOP leaders have kept the door open to a second reconciliation package, with health care among the topics under consideration for inclusion — especially if a bipartisan deal does not come together. 

Republicans were also quick to note that Trump’s move complicates things for Thune, who has the already difficult task of keeping the chamber together heading into an election year. 

“We have seven or eight votes that are up for grabs on every bill. … It hurts Thune, which therefore hurts Trump because he’s the only one that protects Trump from some of these senators,” the GOP operative said. “You can’t expect John Thune to deliver anything when all you’re doing is attacking his members. At some point, they don’t listen to John Thune because you’re attacking them anyway.” 

The majority of the Senate GOP doesn’t share Trump’s feelings about Cassidy, who is held in high regard across much of the conference, one senior GOP aide noted. 

“It’d be a little different if he was one of the more unpopular guys, but that’s not the case,” they said.

However, multiple Republicans indicated that they believe they have time before Cassidy becomes a potential legislative impediment. The Louisiana primary is not until mid-May, meaning that the incumbent Republican likely has to toe the line with Trump until then. 

That is, however, as long as Cassidy stays in the race, with multiple GOP sources indicating they expect him to do so, especially given his financial prowess. He must decide by the Feb. 13 filing deadline.

“If he doesn’t run again, you can probably kiss the second reconciliation bill goodbye immediately,” the senior GOP aide said. “If he becomes a ‘lean no’ vote when it comes down to it on the more controversial things, I think things grind to a halt starting in the summer.”

Cassidy, for his part, responded to Letlow’s announcement by writing on the social platform X, “I will continue to do a good job when I win re-election.”

Louisiana political strategist Mary-Patricia Wray, who’s worked with both Republicans and Democrats, said she spoke with the GOP senator at an energy industry event in October where she floated the idea of him running as an independent in the race, but she said the senator indicated he was not interested in pursuing it. Wray said she cut a small check to sponsor a lunch for Senate candidate Julie Emerson but hasn’t contributed money to any other candidates or endorsed a candidate in the Senate race.

Cassidy’s campaign did not respond to a request for comment for this story.

What also remains to be seen is whether the outside financial cavalry comes to the aid of either Cassidy or Letlow. 

The Senate Leadership Fund (SLF), which is run by Thune allies, indicated it may not play in the state as it is focused on the preservation and expansion of the Senate GOP, with Louisiana being considered a safe red seat. However, Trump’s decision to wade into the contest may force Republicans to spend in a state where their resources could have been used elsewhere. 

“Anything that distracts from our efforts to beat Democrats in November is unhelpful,” Alex Latcham, SLF executive director, said in a statement.

A second GOP operative also noted that the big question for Letlow is how much weight the Trump operation puts behind the endorsement and whether MAGA Inc. pumps funds into the race.  

The picture is especially complicated in Louisiana, where grassroots Republicans have soured on Cassidy ever since his vote to convict Trump. Some major donors, though, have rallied around the Republican senator — at least for now. 

“There’s a class of about 40 to 50 really wealthy donors in this state, and we all know who they are, and the president knows who they are, and they’re going to be expected to line up,” or at the very least start spreading around donations, said Republican consultant Scott Wilfong.

Cassidy has about $11 million in the bank as of the end of the year.
